coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/KNbO3 hybrid composites with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity toward dye waste-water degradation and hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Zepeng
Wang, Jing
Tang, Jianhua
Shu, Xiaoqing
Liu, Xudong
Zhang, Zhaohong
Wang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new visible-light driven photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/KNbO3 composite, is successfully prepared via hydrothermal and mixed-calcination methods Multiple techniques, such as X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, are applied to investigate the morphologies, structures and optical properties of the prepared CQDs/KNbO3 composites. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/KNbO3 composites is evaluated via degradation of crystal violet dye as target organic pollutant with simultaneous hydrogen production under visible-light irradiation. The results show that the CQDs/KNbO3 composites exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity than the KNbO3. It is ascribed to the presence of the CQDs as co-catalyst on the surface of KNbO3 particles, forming much more active sites for trapping electrons and promoting the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. In addition, the CQDs can absorb visible-lights and emit the ultraviolet-lights to activate the wide band-gap KNbO3. The study of reusability shows that the CQDs/KNbO3 can be effectively recycled for four repetitive cycles and maintain a higher level of photocatalytic activity. In conclusion, the present work designs an effectively visible-light driven photocatalyst, which can be applied in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants with simultaneous hydrogen evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Wide band-gap KNbO3
Hybrid composites
Visible-light photocatalytic degradation
Simultaneous hydrogen evolution

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs/KNbO3 composite as visible-light photocatalyst
Photocatalytic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution
Absorption and emission spectra of CQDs
XRD, SEM, TEM, BET, XPS, EDX, FT-IR, UV-vis and PL of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3
Photocatalytic activity of prepared CQDs/KNbO3 over photocatalytic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution
Effect of used times on visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution caused by CQDs/KNbO3
Mechanism and process of visible-light photocatalystic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular structure diagram of crystal violet.
The absorption spectrum of CQDs in aqueous solution.
The emission spectra of CQDs in aqueous solution under excitation of different wavelength lights.
XRD patterns of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 with different mass proportions.
SEM images of prepared KNbO3 (a) and CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) (b).
TEM images of prepared CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) (a: 20nm, b: 10nm and c: 5.0nm).
XPS spectra of CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) composite. a: full spectra; b: K 2p; c: Nb 3d; d: O 1s; e: C 1s.
EDX spectra of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5).
FT-IR spectra of CQDs, KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composite.
UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and plots of (αhν)1/2 versus band-gap energy (b) of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5).
Photoluminescence spectra of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5).
Photocatalytic degradation (a) of crystal violet and hydrogen evolution (b) over as-prepared KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 under visible-light irradiation.
Effect of used times on photocatalytic degradation (a) of crystal violet with simultaneous hydrogen evolution (b) of CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) as photocatalyst.
Principle and mechanism on photocatalystic degradation of crystal violet with simultaneous hydrogen evolution caused by CQDs/KNbO3 as photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/KNbO3 hybrid composites with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity toward dye waste-water degradation and hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new visible-light driven photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/KNbO3 composite, is successfully prepared via hydrothermal and mixed-calcination methods Multiple techniques, such as X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, are applied to investigate the morphologies, structures and optical properties of the prepared CQDs/KNbO3 composites. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/KNbO3 composites is evaluated via degradation of crystal violet dye as target organic pollutant with simultaneous hydrogen production under visible-light irradiation. The results show that the CQDs/KNbO3 composites exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity than the KNbO3. It is ascribed to the presence of the CQDs as co-catalyst on the surface of KNbO3 particles, forming much more active sites for trapping electrons and promoting the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. In addition, the CQDs can absorb visible-lights and emit the ultraviolet-lights to activate the wide band-gap KNbO3. The study of reusability shows that the CQDs/KNbO3 can be effectively recycled for four repetitive cycles and maintain a higher level of photocatalytic activity. In conclusion, the present work designs an effectively visible-light driven photocatalyst, which can be applied in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants with simultaneous hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, with the rapid development of industry, the environmental pollution has become a very serious problem in the society [1]. In recent decades, the water quality has been deteriorating, which has seriously destroyed the health of the residents, and has caused great harm to the environment. It has been reported that 783 million people do not have access to clean water and almost 2.5 billion do not have access to adequate sanitation [2]. Therefore, 6–8 million people die annually due to diseases related to the poor quality of water. Consequently, more and more scholars began to pay attention to the restoration of water pollution. Nevertheless, the conventional methods of waste water treatment, such as carbon adsorption, coagulation and activated sludge, have some deficiencies in the actual treatment process, making these pollutants difficult to be decomposed completely, and sometimes also causing secondary pollution. They also suffer from high capital and operating costs [3]. Thus, the new and feasible treatment method of organic waste water has become an important topic at the present stage, and many scientists have put forward reasonable solutions to solve these problems.

As we all know, for many conventional methods the organic pollutants in water were only dealt with to achieve the purpose of protecting the environment and not utilized appropriately as available resources. Apparently, it becomes a waste of resources. That is, these recalcitrant pollutants should play a role in the reuse of resources, while dealing with them, producing the renewable energy resources-Hydrogen. Hydrogen (H2) is a clean and high-density energy carrier proposed to solve the energy shortage and environmental pollution issues [4]. In recent years, photocatalysis technology, offers a “green” programme for degradation of organic pollutants (especially some azodyes) and obtaining ideal hydrogen energy from water [1,5–7], which has been recognized as a potential strategy for environmental remediation and at the same time as an opportunity for the production of clean energy. The traditional semiconductor photocatalysts are mainly metal oxides, sulfides and metal inorganic salts, such as TiO2, ZnO, CdS and so on. Among them, TiO2 is the most typical representive and has been extensively used as a semiconductor photocatalyst. Unfortunately, the relatively wide band-gap of TiO2 (3.0–3.2eV) greatly limited its application, because it is activated solely by ultraviolet light with a wave length of no longer than 387.5nm [8]. Hence, various strategies such as combination with metal/noble metal materials, coupling with other semiconductors and constructing various morphologies have been developed to functionalize TiO2 photocatalyst [9]. In recent years, the development and construction of new photocatalytic materials with good stability and high catalytic efficiency have become a key issue in the treatment of water pollution [6,7,10–14]. As one kind of widely used material, the application of niobium acid salt has received extensive attention. ABO3 (where A is a metal, B is a second metal and O is oxygen) is a ternary transition metal oxide with perovskite structure and often exhibits unique physicochemical as well as photocatalytic properties [15]. Alkaline niobates containing Nb5+ cations are typical ultraviolet-responsive photocatalysts with empty d orbital in the conduction-band [16]. First of all, KNbO3 is nontoxic and highly stable under light illumination and the band-gap (Ebg ≥3.3eV) of KNbO3 is slightly wider than that (Ebg ≥3.2eV) of TiO2. In addition, their high crystallinity and abundance of surface active sites are conducive to photo-absorption and redox reactions, and allow them to efficiently gather organic pollutants in wastewater. However, the photocatalytic acticity of the pure KNbO3 semiconductor is limited due to low efficiency of light absorption and high recombination probability of photo-generated electron-hole pairs.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new member of carbon material family with sizes below 10nm, were first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 [17] and have gradually become a rising star. As an attractive material distinct from the metal-based quantum dots, the CQDs not only exhibit chemical inertness, excellent solubility, fine biocompatibility and low toxicity, as well as stable photoluminescence, but also possess other distinct merits, such as interesting pH-dependent, size-dependent and wavelength-dependent of up-conversion and down-conversion photoluminescence. To obtain fluorescent CQDs, tremendous effort has been placed on developing synthetic methods. Top-down approaches have proven to be very effective, and take advantage of the low cost and highly abundant raw materials [18]. Together with above advantages [19], in recent years, CQDs have been successfully applied to chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging and electrocatalysis [20].

Due to the π–π* (CC) and n-π* (CO) transitions, CQDs showed a broad ultraviolet absorption with a tail in the near-visible region [21]. Recently, researchers have found that CQDs exhibit excellent up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) properties, which can transfer the near infrared (NIR) or infrared (IR) light into ultraviolet- and visible-light [22]. That is to say, the CQDs can expand the light utilization range of wide band-gap semiconductor photocatalysts from ultraviolet-light to visible-light range [23]. Furthermore, CQDs are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous and nanocrystalline cores with predominantly graphitic or turbostratic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridised carbon insertions [23]. It can provide the CQDs a special ability with superior electron transfer and reservoir [24]. As a powerful energy-transfer component, CQDs have been used to design some photocatalysts with enhanced activity by combining with semiconductor nanoparticles [25], such as TiO2 [26–29], CdS [30], Fe2O3 [31,32], g-C3N4 [33], Ag3PO4 [25], Cu2O [34] and Bi2WO6 [35]. However, the related functional mechanism of CQDs for photocatalytic activity improvement as well as the binding form between CQDs and semiconductors are far from clear.

Considering the superiorities of KNbO3 and CQDs above mentioned, CQDs as up-conversion luminescence agent and co-catalyst can be applied to modify the KNbO3, which is conducive to providing much more ultraviolet-light to activate the wide band-gap KNbO3 and forming more active sites. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report regarding CQDS modified KNbO3 materials and their application in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants with simultaneous hydrogen evolution. Therefore, in this work, as an excellent visible-light-driven photocatalyst, the CQDs/KNbO3 was successfully prepared by hydrothermal and mixed-calcination methods. The visible-light photocatalytic performance of CQDs/KNbO3 was investigated through the photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye with simultaneous hydrogen evolution. Moreover, the reusability of CQDs/KNbO3 as photocatalyst was also examined in four times repeat cycle experiments. It is wished that the CQDs/KNbO3 as an effectively visible-light driven photocatalyst could be applied in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants with simultaneous hydrogen evolution.

Niobium pentoxide (Nb2O5) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) (analytical grade, Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co, Ltd, China) were used to prepare the potassium niobate (KNbO3). L-ascorbic acidand glycol and glycol (analytical grade, National Medicines Corporation Ltd, China) were obtained to prepare the carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Crystal violet (analytical grade, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co, Ltd, China) was used as target pollutant and electron donor (molecular structure was shown in Fig. 1 ). All the reagents were of analytical purity grade, and were directly used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/KNbO3 composite as visible-light photocatalyst

ce:section.ce:para: KNbO3 particles were prepared by using a hydrothermal method following the work reported by Yan et al. [36]. 3.57g Nb2O5 and 37.69g KOH were added into 19mL distilled water, and then was stirred vigorously for 30min. The mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160°C for 12h. The products were washed with distilled water and ethanol several times, and dried at 80°C overnight. Finally, the products was calcined at 400°C for 1.0h, after fully grinding, the KNbO3 particles were obtained.

The preparation procedure of CQDs is via simple hydrothermal method. First, 1.6g L-ascorbic acid, 15mL glycol and 25mL triple distilled water were mixed in a beaker with a magnetic blender, then the mixture was equally distributed into two high pressure Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (45mL) and heated at 160°C for 70min without any further passivation [37]. The product was left to cool naturally, then CQDs were obtained and used directly.

100mg CQDs was dissolved in 100mL deionized water, the concentration of the CQDs solution was 1.0mg/mL. CQDs/KNbO3 composites were prepared by a simple route, in which 0.50g KNbO3 particles were dispersed in a 75mL CQDs solution (1.0mg/mL) with continuous stirring for 30min at room temperature, and dried at 80°C for 8.0h. The different molar ratios of CQDs/KNbO3 composites were prepared by changing the concentration of CQDs solution from 0.0mg/mL, to 2.0mg/mL at 0.5mg/mL interval, which were labeled as CQDs/KNbO3 (0.0/0.5), CQDs/KNbO3 (0.5/0.5), CQDs/KNbO3 (1.0/0.5), CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) and CQDs/KNbO3 (2.0/0.5), respectively.

The prepared KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D-8, Bruker-axs, Germany) using Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation in the range of 2θ from 10° to 70°, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-5610LV, Hitachi Corporation, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100, Hitachi Corporation, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher, USA) on a Kratos Axis Ultra spectrometer using a monochromatized Al Ka radiation, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX, JEOL JSM-5610LV, Hitachi Corporation, Japan), Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR, 580 BIR spectrophotometer, Perkin-Elmer Corporation, USA), UV–vis absorption (UV-vis, UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan) and photoluminescence (PL, RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the samples were determined from nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms determined at liquid nitrogen temperature on an automatic analyzer (Autosorb-iQ-MP, Quantachrome, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalyst were evaluated via the degradation of crystal violet dye as target organic pollutant in an aqueous solution under visible-light irradiation. A 300W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter provided visible-light irradiation. In photocatalytic degradation experiment, 50mg of prepared photocatalyst was added into 50mL crystal violet dye aqueous solution (10mg/L) and added in the Pyrex photocatalytic reactors. Prior to visible-light irradiation, the dispersion was stirred in the dark about 30min to ensure absorption-desorption equilibrium between crystal violet dye and the used photocatalyst. Then, the suspension was carried out under 300W xenon lamp irradiation with a cut-off filter (LX-300, Deruifeng Hardware Electrical Appliance Businesses, China) for 5.0h, the distance between light and liquid surface is 20cm. For visible-light irradiation, a cut-off filter (L42) was fitted to the aforementioned light source (420nm<λ<800nm). During the photodegradation process, 3.00mL suspension was sampled at certain time intervals and centrifuged at 13000rpm for 3min to remove the CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalyst for analysis. The concentration of crystal violet dye samples were analyzed on an UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-3310, Hitachi Corporation, Japan) at 582nm wavelength for crystal violet. The degradation efficiency (DE) was calculated according to the following equation,
DE (%)=(1-C/C0)×100%
Where C0 and C represent the adsorption equilibrium concentration of solution prior to irradiation and the concentration after certain interval irradiation time, respectively.

The visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen production experiment was conducted in a 500mL Pyrex reactor. The headspace of the reactor was connected to an inverted burette which was filled with water at atmospheric pressure, which allowed the measurement of the hydrogen production. In photocatalytic reaction, 50mg of photocatalyst was dispersed using a magnetic stirrer in crystal violet dye aqueous solution (50 mg·L−1). Before hydrogen production experiment, the system was refilled with argon for 30min to remove the air inside and to ensure that the reaction system was under anaerobic condition. And then, the suspension was exposed to visible-light irradiation for 5.0h under magnetic stirring. A 300W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter provided visible-light irradiation, the distance between light and liquid surface is 20cm. The formation of hydrogen was confirmed by injecting 0.50mL of the reactor headspace gas in a gas chromatograph (GC-8A, MS-5A column, TCD, Ar Carrier, Shimadazu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and emission spectra of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CQDs were dispersed in 100mL deionized water, and then the UV–vis absorption spectrum was determined and the result was given in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that the prepared CQDs in aqueous solution display strong absorption in the ultraviolet (<400nm) region. In addition, being similar to the properties of other semiconductor quantum dots, many absorption peaks can also be found in the visible (400–600nm) region. It indicates that the as-obtained CQDs exhibit a broad UV–vis absorption, which was attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions. It provides a possibility for CQDs to utilize visible-light part in solar energy.

For some materials, up-conversion fluorescence emission is an special optical phenomenon wherein the fluorescence emission wavelength is shorter than the used excitation wavelength. It was found that emission spectra of CQDs are divided into two parts (a and b) in Fig. 3 . On the one hand, when the excitation wavelengths from 400nm to 500nm are used to excite the prepared CQDs, the emission peaks appear around 530nm. At this point, the prepared CQDs show the nature of the down-conversion luminescence. And that, the luminescence intensity decreases with the increase of the excitation wavelength in Fig. 3(a). On the other hand, when the excitation wavelengths from 500nm to 600nm are used to excite the prepared CQDs, the prepared CQDs demonstrate obvious up-conversion luminescence property and the emission peaks appear in the ultraviolet-light region from 300 to 450nm in Fig. 3(b). It also can be seen that the luminescence intensity decreases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. Apparently, the ultraviolet-light emitted by the CQDs can excite the wide band-gap KNbO3 to form more electron-hole (e− − h+) pairs and thus exhibit much powerful oxidation and reduction capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, SEM, TEM, BET, XPS, EDX, FT-IR, UV-vis and PL of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3
ce:section.ce:para: The patterns of prepared KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composites were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and the corresponding diffraction spectra were shown in Fig. 4 . It can be obviously found that all given XRD patterns of prepared KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composites are similar to the JCPDS card 32-0832 of KNbO3 without any interference peaks [38]. The diffraction angles at 2θ=22.04°, 31.56°, 45.02°, 51.04°, 56.04° and 65.82° correspond well with (110), (111), (220), (221), (311) and (222) planes, respectively, and no other crystalline phases can be detected. The spectra for all the CQDs/KNbO3 composites are similar to that for pure KNbO3, indicating that the lattice structure of KNbO3 did not be changed even in the presence of CQDs. In addition, there is also no observable shift of the characteristic diffraction peaks in the spectra for all CQDs/KNbO3 composites, suggesting that the prepared CQDs were not incorporated into the KNbO3 lattice. Otherwise, with the increase of CQDs concentration in the CQDs/KNbO3 composites, the diffraction peaks also increase. Besides, the characteristic peak of carbon at 26° is too weak to be observed, due to the small quantity and particle size of CQDs, as well as their high dispersion [39,40].

The morphology and microstructure of the prepared KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the results were displayed in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 , respectively, which reveals that the composites is consist of KNbO3 and CQDs. In Fig. 5(a), it can be found that there are a lot of cube-like crystal particles ranging from 200nm to 250nm in side length and the surface is smooth and bright, which should belong to the typical crystal form of micron-sized KNbO3 particles. In Fig. 5(b), the surface of CQDs/KNbO3 composites is obviously rough and dark, which demonstrates that the prepared CQDs may be mainly deposited on the surface of KNbO3 particles. The prepared CQDs on the surface KNbO3 particles are difficult to be discerned through SEM due to their very small size and high dispersion.

The microstructure of the CQD/KNbO3 composites was further investigated by TEM analysis. Fig. 6 shows the typical TEM image of CQDs/KNbO3 composites with different proportional scales. It is obvious found that a lot of dark dots belonging to CQDs are dispersed and attached on the surface of KNbO3, and that the density of CQDs is not so high. It implies that the CQDs have been successfully attached on the surface of KNbO3 and the intimate integration has been constructed. To further confirm the formation of the composite structures, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded and the result was also given. The obtained HRTEM image clearly demonstrated that the CQDs were monodisperse with a spherical shape diameter of 4–9nm and revealed that the CQDs have an interplanar spacing of 0.17nm. At the same time, the lattice spacing of prepared KNbO3 around 0.404nm can be observed, which agree well with the crystallographic (110) spacing of KNbO3 (JCPDS 32-0832) [38]. The above results further demonstrate that the prepared CQDs have been modified on the surface of KNbO3 to construct the CQDs/KNbO3 composites.

BET surface area (SBET) is directly associated with the efficiency of photocatalysis and can be evaluated by nitrogen adsorption and desorption measurement. The determined results showed that the prepared KNbO3 sample has relatively small BET surface area of 3.926m2 g−1, but after modified by the CQDs the BET surface area is obviously increased. For CQDs/KNbO3 (0.5/0.5), CQDs/KNbO3 (1.0/0.5), CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) and CQDs/KNbO3 (2.0/0.5), their BET surface areas are 4.083m2 g−1, 4.256m2 g−1, 4.742m2 g−1 and 4.191m2 g−1, respectively. It can be found that the BET surface area of samples firstly increases with the increase of mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 (from 0.5/0.5 to 1.5/0.5), and then slightly decreases (from 1.5/0.5 to 2.0/0.5) with the further increase of CQDs. Apparently, a proper amount of CQDs can increase the unevenness of KNbO3 surface, thus increasing the BET surface area. However, loading excessive CQDs on the surface of KNbO3 causes to aggregation, thereby leads to the decrease of BET surface area. Meanwhile, the prepared KNbO3 shows a relatively wide distribution of the pore size, including small mesopores of 15nm and lager mesopores of 35nm.

Furthermore, XPS determination was carried out to investigate the components and states of the CQDs/KNbO3 composite and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7(a), the binding energies at 530.7eV, 292.2eV, 284.6eV and 206.1eV are corresponding to the characteristic peaks of O 1s, K 2P, C 1s and Nb 3d, respectively, which confirms the existence of K, Nb, O and C elements in prepared CQDs/KNbO3 sample. Fig. 7(b) displays the high resolution XPS spectrum of K and K 2p3/2 and K 2p1/2 are located at 292.2eV and 295.1eV, respectively. Fig. 7(c) displays that the binding energies for Nb 3d5/2 and Nb 3d3/2 are located at 206.1eV and 208.8eV, respectively; which are similar to the reported values in KNbO3 [22]. Furthermore, in Fig. 7(d) the main peak in O 1s spectrum at 284.6eV is attributed to the CO and OH bonds [37]. Fig. 7(e) displays the peak locate at 284.6eV in XPS spectrum of C 1s, which is assigned to the CC bond with sp2 hybridised orbital and reveals the presence of CQDs in prepared CQDs/KNbO3 sample.

To further confirm the successful loading of the CQDs on the surface of KNbO3, the EDX spectra of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composites were determined and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 8 . In Fig. 8(a), the strong peaks of K, Nb and O elements can be observed easily, which indicates that these elements compose the prepared KNbO3. Fig. 8(b) not only displays the main peaks of K, Nb and O elements, but also gives the peak of C element, which indicates that the CQDs have been attached to the surface of KNbO3 to construct the CQDs/KNbO3 composites. Moreover, the compositional analyses also display the elemental contents of K, Nb, O and C in KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3, which are consistent with the results of XPS spectra.

The determination of FT-IR spectra is carried out to further confirm the presence of CQDs in CQDs/KNbO3 composites. Fig. 9 presents the FT-IR spectra of CQDs, KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composite. Firstly, it can be clearly observed that the CQDs shows a broad absorption peak spanning 3150–3450cm−1, indicating that the surface of CQDs is rich in −OH groups. The sharp peaks at 1596.7cm−1 and 1401.1cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bond and that the absorption band arond 1095.3cm−1 is associated with stretching vibrations of CO bond [41]. In addition, the CQDs/KNbO3 displayed similar spectrum with that of KNbO3, indicating the introduction of CQDs hardly changes the chemical structure of KNbO3. In addition, the peak at 3197.9cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibrations of OH of adsorbed water molecules. Meanwhile, an obvious absorption peak at 1660.4cm−1 can be ascribed to deformation vibrations of the OH groups of adsorbed water molecules. Otherwise, the absorption peak at 632.9cm−1 is ascribed to the vibrations of NbONb, which implies the existence of KNbO3. Furthermore, the absorption peak located at 1458.7cm−1 can be found for the CQDs/KNbO3 composites, which is attributed to the stretching vibrations of −COO− and thus reveals the existence of CQDs in CQDs/KNbO3 composites. In conclusion, the FT-IR results preliminarily indicate that CQDs have been successfully deposited on the KNbO3.

Photoabsorption is one of the key factors affecting the performance of photocatalysts and is usually evaluated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. The photoabsorption properties of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 composites were shown in Fig. 10 . In Fig. 10(a), the KNbO3 exhibits a strong absorption band in the region from 260nm to 400nm. This absorption originates from the charge transfer response of KNbO3 from valence-band (VB) to conduction-band (CB). For CQDs/KNbO3 (from 0.5/0.5 to 2.0/0.5) composites, the absorption edges all showed an apparent red-shift compared with that of KNbO3. With the increase of the CQDs content, the movement of absorption range is gradually aggravated toward long wavelength, which means that the photoabsorption in visible-light range has been enhanced. The above results suggest that CQDs may play an important role in utilizing visible-light and the enhanced light absorption can effectively excite the KNbO3 to form more electron-hole (e− − h+) pairs and thus lead to an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the highly photocatalytic activity of wide band-gap KNbO3 can be maintained [42]. The corresponding Kubelka-Munk transformed spectra are shown in Fig. 10(b), where the band-gap energy can be estimated from the tangent to the plot of (αhν)1/2 versus radiation energy (hν). The calculated band-gaps of the KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 are 3.30eV and 3.25eV, respectively. Apparently, due to the presence of CQDs the band-gap of CQDs/KNbO3 becomes slightly smaller than that of KNbO3. It means that a highly photocatalytic performance will be obtained in visible-light photocatalytic reaction [43].

The charge separation is a most key factor, which essentially determines the efficiency of photocatalysis [44]. To understand the photophysical behaviors of photo generated electron-hole (e− − h+) pairs, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of KNbO3 and CQD/KNbO3 (1.5:0.5) composites are recorded. In general, for any semiconductor photocatalyst a lower PL intensity reveals a lower recombination rate of electron-hole (e− − h+) pairs, and thus displaying a higher photocatalytic performance. Fig. 11 displays the PL spectra of KNbO3 and CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5:0.5) composite excited by 400nm wavelength of light. It is obvious that the emission intensity of CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5:0.5) composite is much lower than that of KNbO3. It indicates that the modification by using CQDs favours the separation of photo-generated electron-hole (e− − h+) pairs in KNbO3 through effective transfer of the electrons from the conduction-band (CB) of KNbO3 to CQDs. It is beneficial to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity of KNbO3. These results further confirm that the CQDs/KNbO3 composite material is a typical and excellent photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation of pollutant with simultaneous hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of prepared CQDs/KNbO3 over photocatalytic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/KNbO3 as photocatalyst, crystal violet dye was chosen as a target organic pollutant to perform the degradation with simultaneous hydrogen evolution under visible-light irradiation. Fig. 12 (a) shows the comparison of photocatalytic activities of the CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalysts with different CQDs contents as well as pure KNbO3 under visible-light irradiation. It is easy to see that, for all photocatalysts, the degradation ratios of crystal violet dye gradually increase with the extension of visible-light irradiation time and the CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalysts all show much higher photocatalytic activity than that of pure KNbO3. Apparently, the presence of CQDs enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the KNbO3 in the degradation of crystal violet dye. In addition, along with the increase of mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 from 0.5/0.5 to 1.5/0.5, the degradation ratio of crystal violet dye gradually increases. When the CQDs/KNbO3 with mass proportion of 1.5/0.5 was used as photocatalyst, after 5.0h irradiation above 70% of crystal violet dye can be degraded. And that, for pure KNbO3 only 41.50% of crystal violet dye is obtained. However, when the mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 is higher than 1.5/0.5, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalyst slightly decreases, which indicates that the photocatalytic activity of KNbO3 photocatalyst can be enhanced by depositing suitable amount of CQDs.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 12(b) a similar regular pattern can be found. That is, the hydrogen evolution amount also increases gradually with the extension of visible-light irradiation time in the presence of prepared photocatalysts. Similarly, the CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalysts with different mass proportions of CQDs and KNbO3 show much higher hydrogen evolution activity than that of pure KNbO3. With the increase of mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 from 0.5/0.5 to 1.5/0.5, the hydrogen evolution amount increases gradually. When the mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 is 1.5/0.5, the hydrogen evolution amount is the highest and achieves 468.72μmol (hg)−1. Nevertheless, the hydrogen evolution amount is only 245.52μmol (hg)−1 for pure KNbO3 in crystal violet dye aqueous solution after 5.0h irradiation. With the same change trend in the photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye, when the mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 is higher than 1.5/0.5, the CQDs/KNbO3 composite shows a slight decrease hydrogen evolution amount. The results indicate that the proper introduction of the CQDs is beneficial for the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalysts. Too many CQDs on the surface of KNbO3 particles would limit the light absorption of KNbO3, leading to lower photocatalytic activity. Similar results can also been found in the literature [45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of used times on visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution caused by CQDs/KNbO3
ce:section.ce:para: The reusability and stability of the photocatalyst are vital to the practical application. To evaluate the reusability and stability of the prepared CQDs/KNbO3 (1.5/0.5) composite as photocatalyst, the recycling reactions are performed for the photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye with simultaneous hydrogen evolution under visible-light irradiation. From Fig. 13 , it can be seen that there are no significant decreases for degradation rate and hydrogen evolution amount within four consecutive cycles at intervals of four hours for each cycle. Therefore, the repetition tests display that the prepared photocatalyst, CQDs/KNbO3, has an excellent stability on photocatalytic activity. As a satisfactory visible-light-driven photocatalyst, the CQDs/KNbO3 can perform the pollutant removal with simultaneous hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and process of visible-light photocatalystic degradation of pollutant (crystal violet dye) with simultaneous hydrogen evolution
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism diagram of CQDs/KNbO3 composite as photocatalyst on the degradation of organic pollutant with simultaneous hydrogen evolution is presented in Fig. 14 . In fact, for achieving effectively photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants with simultaneous hydrogen evolution it is very necessary to choose a wide band-gap semiconductor photocatalyst with more negative conduction-band (CB) and more positive valence-band (VB), which can obtain strong reduction capacity and strong oxidation capacity at the same time. For this reason, KNbO3 should be an ideal and superior semiconductor photocatalyst, because of its wide band-gap (Ebg ≥3.3eV) as well as very negative conduction-band (-0.86eV) and very positive valence-band (+2.44eV). Therefore, KNbO3 is very suitable for the pollutant removal with simultaneous hydrogen evolution. Unfortunately, as a wide band-gap (Ebg ≥3.3eV) semiconductor photocatalyst, the KNbO3 demonstrate high photocatalytic activity only under ultraviolet-light irradiation. Nevertheless, the visible-light (∼48%) and near infrared-light (∼44%) in solar energy spectrum cannot be utilized by KNbO3. It limits the utilization of cheap solar energy in photocatalytic reaction. How to make full use of solar light is an urgent problem to be solved.

In recent years, the unique up-conversion luminescence property and superior electronic conductivity of CQDs have attracted much attention. Therefore, in this work a novel visible-light photocatalystic system, CQDs/KNbO3, was designed for degradation of organic pollutants with simultaneous hydrogen evolution based on the characteristics of CQDs and KNbO3. The CQDs part in CQDs/KNbO3 plays an important role in the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of KNbO3. Firstly, the CQDs as an up-conversion luminescent material can convert the visible-light to ultraviolet-light due to the excellent up-conversion performance. Secondly, because of the excellent electronic conductivity the CQDs can be used as co-catalyst, forming much more active sites on the surface of KNbO3 particles. Then, the KNbO3 is irradiated by the ultraviolet-light from CQDs, the electrons (e−) can be excited from valence-band (VB) to conduction-band (CB) of KNbO3, leaving the holes (h+) on conduction-band (VB) of KNbO3. Normally, these photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) quickly recombine and only a portion of charges could participate in the photocatalytic reaction process. The photo-generated electrons (e−) on conduction-band (CB) of KNbO3 tend to transfer to the CQDs, because the CQDs with delocalized conjugated structure have superior electronic conductivity acting as active sites. The recombination of photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) can be effectively suppressed, thus the photocatalytic activity is enhanced to some extent. Finally, the enriched photo-generated electrons (e−) on the CQDs could easily react with H+ in water to evolve hydrogen (H2). At the same time, the strong oxidizing holes (h+) on conduction-band (VB) of KNbO3 are captured by crystal violet molecules and then oxidize them to form CO2, H2O, NO3 − and SO4 2−. So, the degradation of pollutant with simultaneous hydrogen evolution can be successfully carried out by using the novel photocatalytic system, CQDs/KNbO3, under visible-light irradiation.

The detailed process may be as following:
CQDs/KNbO3 +visible-light (in solar light)→[CQDs]*/KNbO3
[CQDs]*/KNbO3 →CQDs/KNbO3 +ultraviolet-light (from CQDs)
CQDs/KNbO3 +ultraviolet-light (from CQDs)→CQDs/[KNbO3]*
CQDs/[KNbO3]*→e−/CB/KNbO3 +h+/CB/KNbO3
e−/CB/KNbO3/CQDs→e−/CQDs/KNbO3
e−/CQDs/KNbO3 +H+ →H2 +CQDs/KNbO3
h+/CB/KNbO3 +crystal violet dye→D+ →→D++ →→→CO2 +H2O+NO3 − +SO4 2−

In summary, the prepared CQDs were dispersed on the surface of KNbO3 particles via hydrothermal and mixed-calcination methods and then a new visible-light-induced photocatalyst, CQDs/KNbO3, was obtained. The CQDs/KNbO3 photocatalyst exhibited remarkably photocatalytic performance in degradation of crystal violet dye with simultaneous hydrogen evolution under visible-light irradiation. The experimental results showed that the degradation rate of crystal violet dye and hydrogen evolution amount reached 70.00% and 468.72μmol (hg)−1, respectively, when the mass proportion of CQDs and KNbO3 was 1.5:0.5, while they were 41.50% and 245.52μmol (hg)−1, respectively, for pure KNbO3. Apparently, the proper introduction of CQDs as up-conversion luminescence agent and co-catalyst was beneficial for the enhancement of the visible-light photocatalytic performance of KNbO3. In addition, the prepared CQDs/KNbO3 composites can be effectively recycled without apparent deactivation on the photocatalytic activity. It may be valuable to design and develop novel photocatalysts towards production of new energy sources and resolving of environmental issues.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the National Science Foundation of China (21371084), the Key Laboratory Basic Research Foundation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (L2015043), Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Projects (LT2015012), the Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Liaoning Province (L2015205) of China, the Natural Science Foundation of Science and Technology Department of Liaoning Province (201602346) of China and Shenyang municipal science and technology plan project (F16-205-1-06) of China for financial support. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participation in this work.
